# Facet

Facet is a plain HTML, CSS and JS design library. It is one CSS file and one
JS file hosted at a public URL. Any page consumes it with one link tag and
one script tag. No React, no build step, no npm install, never minified.
This file is the full usage guide as plain text, for AI crawlers and
builders. The same content lives rendered at the site root and as CLAUDE.md
in the repo: https://github.com/tanishksharma/Facet

## Features — the complete capability inventory

This list is the inventory: if a capability is not on it, it does not
exist. It matches the Features section on the library homepage.

Distribution: two files, one link + one script tag, public URL, no
framework, no npm, no build step, never minified. Until v1 every project
always gets the latest straight from the web; from v1, frozen /lib/v1/
copies pin old projects and cached updates apply only at page
boundaries. /facet.json is the machine-readable manifest: every theme,
token, class, data attribute, component and API call in one fetch.
The homepage adds tools over it: a Playground (one editable HTML box
rendering live through the real library) and a Cheatsheet (the whole
manifest as one dense filterable screen).

App-ready (PWA): a one-line sw.js stub — importScripts of
/lib/facet-sw.js — gives any project the shared caching strategy:
pages network-first so deploys land on the very next refresh, cache as
the offline fallback, other static assets cached instantly and
revalidated behind.
data-service-worker="/sw.js" on the script tag registers it;
[data-install] buttons show only when the browser offers installation
(facet.install() for programmatic prompts); /templates/manifest.json is
the fill-in manifest with the icon checklist.

Theming: one attribute (data-theme) restyles the whole page including
layout containers; dark mode is its own attribute (data-mode="dark") and
composes with every theme; both attributes work on any element for subtree
theming; system dark preference honoured when the page does not choose;
theme and mode ride the URL (?theme=, ?mode=) so shared links open
identically; one-line switcher buttons via data-theme-switch and
data-mode-toggle; setup by configuration — data attributes on the
facet.js script tag boot a page into its look, and facet.set() changes
theme, mode or any token live at runtime; native scrollbars and form
controls follow via color-scheme and facet.js keeps the theme-color
meta synced so the browser chrome wears the theme; system-native
accent — in browsers
that expose the OS accent (Safari 16.4+, Firefox 103+) the Default
theme's accent-3 rank (links, labels, focus rings, selection) takes the
user's own OS accent like a native app, light and dark, with the ink as
the universal fallback; custom accent as a supported recipe — override
a rank's four tokens in one block, page-wide on :root or per-subtree on
any element, and every component using that rank follows; Style Mixer
on the homepage — a picker per semantic token restyles the page live
over any theme, derives hover/pressed/on-colors, rides the URL (?mix=)
and exports a paste-ready facet.set config block; Skin Lab on the
homepage — every theme in light and dark side by side, the same real
layout from identical markup, one attribute apart.

Tokens: every design decision is a role-named CSS variable — color with
per-theme status colors, type scale, weights, spacing on a 4px base,
container widths, radii, border widths, whisper-flat shadows, motion
durations and easings, focus ring. Ranked accents: the base family plus
accent-1 (the one primary action), accent-2 (every secondary action's
fill) and accent-3 (links, labels, quiet affordances), each with hover,
pressed and on-colors in every theme — components pick ranks, never
colors.

Adapts to the reader: all sizes in rem, so device/browser text-size
settings scale the whole interface; fluid hero type via clamp(); 44px
touch targets; no hover-only interactions; motion with personality —
Lively springs by default, data-motion="calm" for quiet, "off" for
none, prefers-reduced-motion above all of it; seamless page
transitions — data-transition="page" animates cross-page navigation
(old page eases away, new one springs in, URL changes normally), with
a[data-no-transition] per-link opt-out and facet.go(url) for
programmatic moves, instant fallback where unsupported; print base
included.

Base layer: raw semantic HTML looks designed with no classes; one visible
keyboard focus ring everywhere; selection colors, thin scrollbars,
skip-to-content link, placeholder styling; the hidden attribute always
wins; tabular figures on numeric UI.

Components and behaviours: themed layout primitives (container in four
widths, stack in three gaps, full-viewport snap sections); buttons in
three variants and three sizes working on <button> and <a>; icon buttons;
the calculator-grade number input (prefilled example, one-tap clear,
locale-aware grouping, words helper in the active language); labelled
fields with hints and
error states; themed sliders with live readouts; verdict-first result
blocks with aria-live; the data-tip description tooltip on one attribute;
all behaviour self-wires to data attributes; built-in context —
facet.now() for date/time/timezone and facet.location for the visitor's
rough place (configurable, disablable, GPS-upgraded only with existing
permission); JS API: facet.set, facet.now, facet.locate,
facet.numberValue, facet.groupIndian, facet.numberWords.

Internationalised: every built-in string comes from one translation
table (facet.strings), switched with data-language and extendable in one
line; number grouping and words follow the locale — lakh/crore where
that is the norm, million/billion elsewhere — defaulting from
facet.location, overridable with data-numbers. No right-to-left layout,
by design.

Accessible by default: AA contrast in every theme light and dark;
everything keyboard operable; ARIA where semantics fall short; icon-only
controls always labelled; live regions on changing content.

AI-native: operable by agents through the DOM alone; shallow semantic
structure; real buttons and links with accessible names; state in the
URL; stable IDs and data-event action names; this file as the one-fetch
manual; docs descriptions written as AI instructions, identical to source
file comments.

The library site: every component live on one page with variant/state
chips; copy buttons serve the exact HTML serialized from the live DOM;
search, scrollspy sidebar, live theme switching.

## Consume it

    <link rel="stylesheet" href="https://[domain]/lib/facet.css">
    <script src="https://[domain]/lib/facet.js" defer></script>

Replace [domain] with the domain this file is served from. Projects never
copy the files in; they point at the hosted URL and update when the library
does.

## Theming

One attribute on the html tag switches the theme, layout containers
included. Dark mode is a second attribute and composes with every theme.

    <html>                                    Default theme, light
    <html data-mode="dark">                   Default theme, dark
    <html data-theme="sand">                  Sand theme, light
    <html data-theme="sand" data-mode="dark"> Sand theme, dark

Themes: Default (white and black, the accent ranks are the ink itself)
ships first. In browsers that expose the OS accent as a CSS system color
(Safari 16.4+ and Firefox 103+ — Chrome does not support AccentColor),
Default's accent-3 rank takes the user's own OS accent for links,
labels, focus rings and selection, in both modes; everywhere else it
stays the ink, and themed pages always keep their own inks. Sand
(Minecraft desert beige, cactus accent-1) is parked and
working. Velvet (data-theme="velvet") is the neumorphic material
extracted from the shipped inflation app: matte velvet surfaces, one
light source from above, elements raised out of the fabric or carved
into it, gold as the only accent ink, serif display headings, tuned
cushion-press physics that hold in every motion personality. Aero
(data-theme="aero") is the Frutiger Aero era: pale sky ground, glass
controls with the hard gloss line, translucent plastic fills, pill
buttons and inputs, a blue glow on the one primary action; dark is
ocean glass — deep water, lifted sky blue, sea-glass green. Elegant
(data-theme="elegant") is cream and gold: ivory surfaces, umber ink,
gold hairline borders, serif display headings, carved elevation —
panels pressed into the cream, never floated on shadows; its dark twin
is obsidian and gold.

facet.js also reads ?theme= and ?mode= from the URL on load, so a shared
link opens in the sender's theme. Buttons with data-theme-switch="sand"
(empty value = Default) switch the theme live; buttons with
data-mode-toggle flip light and dark. Both write their choice back to the
URL.

Setup by configuration: the facet.js script tag can carry the look —
<script src=".../facet.js" data-theme="sand" data-mode="dark" defer>
boots the page into that configuration before first paint (explicit html
attributes win over it; the URL wins over both). At runtime,
facet.set({...}) applies key-value pairs live with no reload:
facet.set({theme: "sand"}), facet.set({mode: "dark"}),
facet.set({"--accent-1": "#5F9E44"}) to override any token. Attributes
on html stay the source of truth, so custom scripts keep working.

The same attributes work on any element, not just html: put
data-mode="dark" (or "light", or data-theme="sand") on a section and only
that subtree flips — tokens are inherited custom properties, so the
nearest element carrying the attribute wins. A themed subtree paints its
own background and text automatically.

Custom accent, the supported recipe: override a rank's four tokens in
one block and every component using that rank follows, in every state.

    :root {
      --accent-1: #0F6FDE;
      --accent-1-hover: #0B5FC2;
      --accent-1-pressed: #0850A6;
      --on-accent-1: #FFFFFF;
    }

Put the block on :root for the whole page, on any element for a
subtree, or apply it live with facet.set({"--accent-1": "#0F6FDE"}).
Check the on-color still passes AA against your accent. Ranks are the
contract — components never pick raw colors, so this block is the whole
theme surface you need.

## Tokens

Every design decision is a CSS variable named by its role, never its value.
Use tokens, never raw hex or px, in anything built on Facet.

Color: --background, --surface, --text, --text-muted, --border, then
the accent ranks — components pick ranks, never colors:
--accent-1 / -hover / -pressed / --on-accent-1   the one primary action
--accent-2 / -hover / -pressed / --on-accent-2   secondary action fill
--accent-3 / -hover / -pressed / --on-accent-3   links, labels, focus,
                                                 quiet affordances
Status: --success, --warning, --error, --info

Type: --font-heading, --font-body, --font-mono; --text-display, --text-h1,
--text-h2, --text-h3, --text-h4, --text-body, --text-small, --text-caption;
--weight-heading, --weight-body, --weight-strong; --leading-heading,
--leading-body

Spacing (4px base): --space-1 4px, --space-2 8px, --space-3 12px,
--space-4 16px, --space-5 24px, --space-6 32px, --space-7 48px,
--space-8 64px. Container widths: --width-narrow 40rem, --width-default
64rem, --width-wide 80rem.

Shape: --radius-small, --radius-medium, --radius-large, --radius-pill;
--border-width, --border-width-strong

Elevation: --shadow-flat, --shadow-raised, --shadow-floating,
--shadow-overlay (whisper-flat, all of them)

Motion: the personality system. Lively, the default — momentum, weight,
playful spring (--ease-spring is a true linear() spring curve where
supported): --duration-fast 150ms, --duration-normal 280ms,
--duration-slow 450ms, --ease-out, --ease-in-out, --ease-spring.
data-motion="calm" swaps the whole set for short, near-linear, slight
values; data-motion="off" stops animation entirely; both work on any
subtree and through facet.set({motion}). Every transition in the
library reads these tokens — no component hardcodes an easing. All
motion collapses to nothing under prefers-reduced-motion, above any
page choice.

Focus: --focus-ring, --focus-offset. One visible ring everywhere,
keyboard only.

## Reader adaptation

The page bends to the reader at the token layer, so every theme and
component adapts with zero extra code. prefers-contrast: more — muted
text gives up its gray, borders climb toward the ink, the focus ring
thickens, in every theme and mode. Forced colors (Windows High
Contrast) — audited; background-shaped elements keep a real border in
the system palette. data-density="compact" on html or any subtree (or
the density script-tag/config key) tightens the whole spacing scale.
.visually-hidden is screen-reader-only text. Text roles .text-display,
.text-h1, .text-h2, .text-h3, .text-h4, .text-body, .text-small,
.text-caption put the type scale on any element without inline styles.
And rem sizing everywhere means the phone's text-size setting scales
the entire interface.

## Base styles

Raw semantic HTML already looks like Facet: headings, paragraphs, links,
lists, blockquotes, code and pre, tables, inputs, textareas, selects,
buttons, fieldsets, figures. Also included: focus rings, selection colors,
thin scrollbars, a .skip-link class for the skip-to-content link, and a
print base. Start every page with:

    <a class="skip-link" href="#main">Skip to content</a>

## Components

Each component below: what it is, what it is for, when to use it, then the
copy-paste snippet. The snippet renders in any page that links facet.css.

### Container

What it is: a page-width wrapper with themed padding. It centers content,
caps its width, and keeps consistent side padding from the spacing tokens.
What it is for: giving every page the same horizontal rhythm, driven by
tokens, so a theme flip restyles the layout too, not just the components
inside it. When to use it: put it directly on the top-level semantic tag of
any ordinary page — main, header, footer, section. Use .container-narrow
for prose, forms and calculators, .container for most pages,
.container-wide for dashboards, .container-full for edge-to-edge strips
that keep side padding. Never nest a container inside a container.

    <main class="container"> ... </main>

### Stack

What it is: vertical flow with themed gaps. A stack says one thing: these
sit above and below each other, this far apart. What it is for: spacing
content without per-element margins. The gaps come from the spacing tokens,
so a theme change restyles the rhythm of the whole page. When to use it:
.stack is the default for content inside a section. Use .stack-tight for
lines that belong together, like a label and its field. Use .stack-loose
between whole sections of a page.

    <section class="stack">
      <h2>A heading</h2>
      <p>Content flows down with themed gaps.</p>
    </section>

### Row

What it is: things side by side that wrap when they run out of room,
with the gap owned by the row. What it is for: button rows, chip sets,
meta lines, any run of small pieces. When to use it: whenever things
sit beside each other — the row owns the gap, so children stay
margin-free, exactly like the stack owns vertical rhythm. Variants:
.row-tight for close runs, .row-loose for breathing room.

    <div class="row">
      <button class="btn btn-small">Save</button>
      <button class="btn btn-ghost btn-small">Delete</button>
    </div>

### Grid

What it is: an auto-fitting column grid — as many columns as fit, each
at least a card wide, collapsing to one on phones with no breakpoints
written. What it is for: card grids, feature grids, galleries,
dashboards. When to use it: any set of same-shaped pieces; the grid
decides the count, the content decides the size. Variants: .grid-wide
for bigger cards, .grid-tight and .grid-loose for gap.

    <div class="grid">
      <article> card </article>
      <article> card </article>
      <article> card </article>
    </div>

### Switch, checkbox, radio

What it is: the yes/no and pick-one controls — native checkbox and
radio wearing the theme through accent-color, and a switch that is a
checkbox upgraded with one class. What it is for: settings, consents,
filters — checkbox for independent yes/nos, radio for one-of-few with
all options visible, switch for a setting that takes effect
immediately. When to use it: wrap control and text in a
label.check-row so the whole line is the hit target. The switch keeps
checkbox semantics — form value, keyboard, screen readers — the class
only changes the clothes.

    <label class="check-row"><input type="checkbox" class="switch" checked> Sound on</label>
    <label class="check-row"><input type="checkbox"> Email me a copy</label>
    <label class="check-row"><input type="radio" name="plan" checked> Monthly</label>

### Stepper & segmented control

What it is: a quantity control — a real number input between two step
buttons in one pill — and a segmented control, a pill of options where
exactly one is pressed in, radios underneath. What it is for: the
stepper for counts and small adjustments where typing is overkill; the
segmented control for switching between 2–5 views or ranges of the
same thing. When to use it: stepper for ranges of a few dozen at most
(beyond that, the number input); segmented for choices that change
what is shown — not settings that act (the switch) and not navigation
(the tab bar). The stepper buttons call the input's own
stepUp/stepDown so min/max/step rule; the radios keep arrows, form
value and announcements native.

    <div class="stepper" role="group" aria-label="Guests">
      <button type="button" class="stepper-down" aria-label="Fewer">−</button>
      <input type="number" value="2" min="1" max="12" aria-label="Guests">
      <button type="button" class="stepper-up" aria-label="More">+</button>
    </div>

    <fieldset class="segmented" aria-label="Range">
      <label><input type="radio" name="range" checked><span>1Y</span></label>
      <label><input type="radio" name="range"><span>5Y</span></label>
    </fieldset>

### Fields: input, textarea, select, search

What it is: the labelled field family — one pattern (label.field >
.field-label + control + .field-hint) wrapped around every kind of
control: text input, textarea, select (with a token-colored chevron,
no image files), and a capsule search field. What it is for: every
form. The label is real, so clicking it focuses the control and
readers announce it; for errors put .field-invalid on the field and
swap the hint for a .field-error line. When to use it: any time a
control needs a name — which is always.

    <label class="field">
      <span class="field-label">Project name</span>
      <input type="text" placeholder="Untitled">
      <span class="field-hint">Shown on the invoice header</span>
    </label>

### File upload & date input

What it is: the native file and date controls, themed and kept native
— the file input's button dresses as a secondary action while
drag-drop, keyboard and the form value stay the browser's; the date
input keeps its native picker with the accent coloring its highlight
and color-scheme flipping it in dark mode. When to use it: always
these before any custom uploader or calendar, wrapped in the same
label.field pattern as every other control.

    <label class="field">
      <span class="field-label">Statement PDF</span>
      <input type="file" accept=".pdf">
    </label>
    <label class="field">
      <span class="field-label">Start date</span>
      <input type="date">
    </label>

### Card

What it is: the grouped-surface primitive — a padded, rounded panel a
breath above the page. What it is for: one thing per card: a product,
a stat, a setting group, a summary. When to use it: .card for most
grouping; .card-outlined when many cards sit close and shadows would
pile up; a clickable card is an <a class="card card-clickable"> — the
whole surface is the link, it lifts on hover and presses on click.
Never put more than one primary action inside a card that is itself
clickable. Velvet raises cards out of the fabric; Elegant carves them
in; Aero glosses them.

    <article class="card">
      <strong>Monthly summary</strong>
      <p>One thing per card.</p>
    </article>
    <a class="card card-clickable" href="/report">Open the report →</a>

### Modal, drawer, popover

What it is: overlays built on the platform's own machinery — modal and
drawer are <dialog> (focus trap, Esc, inert page for free), popover is
the popover attribute (top layer, light dismiss, no JS at all). What
it is for: modal for a decision that blocks the page; drawer for a
side panel of details or filters; popover for small floating bits.
When to use it: open a dialog from any button whose aria-controls
names it — facet.js calls showModal; close with a form method="dialog"
button inside, Esc, or a backdrop click. Popovers need only
popovertarget on the button. The app-kit .sheet stays the settings
panel for app pages; the drawer is the document-world sibling.

    <button class="btn" aria-controls="confirm">Delete…</button>
    <dialog class="modal" id="confirm">
      <p>Delete this report?</p>
      <form method="dialog" class="row">
        <button class="btn btn-primary">Delete</button>
        <button class="btn">Keep it</button>
      </form>
    </dialog>

    <button class="btn" popovertarget="note">Toggle popover</button>
    <div popover id="note">Light-dismisses on any outside click.</div>

### Accordion, toast, dropdown menu

What it is: the accordion is native <details class="accordion">
dressed (hairline rows, rotating chevron; give siblings the same name
attribute and the browser keeps exactly one open); the toast is
facet.toast(message, kind) — a transient pill that says one thing and
leaves after four seconds, announced politely (kinds success /
warning / error / info color the dot); the dropdown is
details.dropdown whose summary wears button clothes and whose
.dropdown-menu holds real buttons and links, closed by outside
clicks, Esc, or picking an item. When to use it: never a toast for a
question (dialog) and never a dropdown for navigation (links).

    <details class="accordion" name="faq">
      <summary>How is the rate calculated?</summary>
      <p>From the last twelve months.</p>
    </details>

    facet.toast("Report saved", "success")

    <details class="dropdown">
      <summary class="btn">More actions</summary>
      <div class="dropdown-menu">
        <button type="button">Export CSV</button>
        <button type="button" class="dropdown-danger">Delete</button>
      </div>
    </details>

### Tabs, breadcrumb, pagination

What it is: the wayfinding trio. Tabs are real ARIA tabs (role=
tablist/tab/tabpanel; click or arrow keys; accent underline; facet.js
wires panels and roving tabindex) for two-to-five in-page views.
Breadcrumb is the path to here — nav.breadcrumb > ol of links with
quiet separators, current page unlinked with aria-current="page".
Pagination is numbered page links in 44px squares, current page
filled in accent-1, edges disabled with aria-disabled on a span.
When to use which: tabs switch views in place; the tab bar navigates
an app; the segmented control is a form value; breadcrumb and
pagination are links, never buttons.

    <div class="tabs" role="tablist" aria-label="Views">
      <button role="tab" aria-selected="true" aria-controls="pane-a" id="tab-a">Overview</button>
      <button role="tab" aria-selected="false" aria-controls="pane-b" id="tab-b">Activity</button>
    </div>
    <div role="tabpanel" id="pane-a" aria-labelledby="tab-a">…</div>
    <div role="tabpanel" id="pane-b" aria-labelledby="tab-b" hidden>…</div>

    <nav class="breadcrumb" aria-label="You are here">
      <ol><li><a href="/">Library</a></li><li><span aria-current="page">Here</span></li></ol>
    </nav>

    <nav class="pagination" aria-label="Pages">
      <a href="?page=1" aria-current="page">1</a> <a href="?page=2">2</a>
    </nav>

### Nav link

What it is: the navigation link with its three states designed —
quiet by default, named on hover, plainly marked when it is the page
you are on (aria-current="page"). What it is for: headers, sidebars,
footers. When to use it: every navigational link in chrome; in
content, plain underlined links stay the rule.

    <nav aria-label="Main">
      <a class="nav-link" href="/" aria-current="page">Dashboard</a>
      <a class="nav-link" href="/reports">Reports</a>
    </nav>

### Table

What it is: the data table — muted header row, zebra striping, a
hover that names the row you are reading, right-aligned tabular
numbers via .num. What it is for: real records: transactions, users,
line items. When to use it: data with columns; one-field lists go in
list markup. Wrap wide tables in .table-scroll so they scroll
horizontally on phones (the wrapper law allows it: it enables
scrolling).

    <div class="table-scroll">
      <table class="table">
        <thead><tr><th scope="col">Month</th><th scope="col" class="num">Amount</th></tr></thead>
        <tbody><tr><td>April</td><td class="num">12,480</td></tr></tbody>
      </table>
    </div>

### Chart

What it is: a line chart drawn by facet.chart(el, {points, projectFrom,
events, format}) into a .chart div — data line in accent-1 (2.5px,
dashed from projectFrom on), dots ringed in the page background, grid
on the border token, muted axis text, event markers as faint dashed
verticals labelled in a band above the plot, and a drag crosshair that
reads the nearest value, dropped on pointer lift. points is
[{x, y, label?}]. Colors are all tokens, so theme and mode switches
restyle a live chart with no redraw; velvet turns the line gold via
accent-1 automatically. The SVG draws at ~1 unit per pixel so type
reads true; the surface is touch-action: none (gesture law). Put the
chart in a .chart-card — a well in velvet, a quiet panel elsewhere.
update(next) redraws with new data.

    <div class="chart-card"><div class="chart" id="prices"></div></div>
    <script>
      facet.chart(document.querySelector("#prices"), {
        points: [{x: 2024, y: 100}, {x: 2025, y: 106}, {x: 2026, y: 112}],
        events: [{x: 2025, label: "Rate cut"}],
      });
    </script>

### Badge, chip, avatar, progress

What it is: four tiny signals. Badge: a status word in a tinted pill —
.badge plus .badge-success / -warning / -error / -info. Chip: a small
toggleable pill button; aria-pressed carries its state and facet.js
toggles a bare chip on click (chips wired to data attributes keep
their own handlers). Avatar: a round face — an image or initials on
the quiet fill; .avatar-large for bigger. Progress: the native
<progress> themed, accent-1 fill on a quiet track. Badges state, they
never act; avatars carry the person's name in alt or aria-label;
determinate progress needs value and max with words nearby.

    <span class="badge badge-success">Paid</span>
    <button class="chip" aria-pressed="false">Travel</button>
    <span class="avatar" aria-label="Tanishk Sharma">TS</span>
    <progress value="70" max="100"></progress>

### Skeleton, spinner, empty state

What it is: the three honest ways to show absence. Skeleton: a
shimmering placeholder — put .skeleton on blocks sized like the
content it stands in for; mark the group aria-hidden. Spinner: a
small accent ring for indeterminate waits — give the row role=status
and words; under reduced motion the ring stands still and the words
do the talking. Empty state: .empty — a dashed quiet zone that names
what would be here and offers the one action that fills it. Prefer
skeletons over spinners whenever the shape is known; an empty state
is content, not an error.

    <div class="stack-tight" aria-hidden="true">
      <span class="skeleton" style="width: 60%;">&nbsp;</span>
      <span class="skeleton" style="width: 90%; height: 5rem;">&nbsp;</span>
    </div>
    <p class="row" role="status"><span class="spinner" aria-hidden="true"></span> Loading…</p>
    <div class="empty">
      <strong>No reports yet</strong>
      <button class="btn btn-primary btn-small">New report</button>
    </div>

### Flagship link

What it is: the big confident link — heading voice, strong accent
underline, an arrow that leans into the journey on hover. What it is
for: the one onward step at the end of a section. When to use it: at
most one or two per screen; ordinary references stay plain underlined
links. Always an <a> to a real URL, never a button in costume.

    <a class="flagship-link" href="/report">See the full report</a>

### Icons

What it is: forty 24x24 line glyphs at a 1.5px stroke with round
caps, carried as markup inside facet.js — no image files, no sprite
fetch. Write <svg data-icon="search"></svg> and facet.js fills it;
icons ride currentColor and scale with the surrounding text.
Decorative by default (aria-hidden); an aria-label on the svg makes
it meaningful. Extend: facet.icons.rocket = '<path d="..."/>' then
facet.icon(). The set: search close check plus minus arrow-right
arrow-left arrow-up arrow-down chevron-right chevron-left chevron-up
chevron-down menu more settings home user users heart star bookmark
bell calendar clock download upload share external link copy edit
trash filter eye lock mail globe image file folder sun moon info
warning help play pause.

    <button class="btn btn-icon" aria-label="Search">
      <svg data-icon="search"></svg>
    </button>

### Parallax & idle motion

What it is: alive by default. data-parallax="depth" registers an
element with facet.motion — it drifts up to depth pixels behind the
desktop pointer, the scroll's momentum (a velocity kick), or the
device tilt where no permission gate blocks it (iOS gates it;
facet.motion.setMode("tilt") asks). The idle-* classes (.idle-float,
.idle-sway, .idle-pulse) give resting elements a slow pulse; they
animate the separate translate/rotate/scale properties so they
compose with the engine's transform instead of colliding — the
parallax exclusion, solved structurally. Never put data-parallax on
elements with their own transform physics (velvet lift/press, the
pager). Calm stills idle life; data-motion="off" and
prefers-reduced-motion still everything.

    <article class="card" data-parallax="10">Drifts gently</article>
    <img class="idle-float" src="hero.png" alt="…">

### Snap section

What it is: full-viewport scroll-snap areas. The .snap wrapper scrolls one
full screen at a time and each .snap-section centers its content at a
readable width. What it is for: the calculator page skeleton — intro
screen, then inputs, then results — and any page that tells one story one
screen at a time. When to use it: single-purpose pages with a clear
screen-by-screen flow. Not for ordinary documents; normal pages just
scroll. The .snap wrapper exists to enable scrolling, which is the one
reason the wrapper law allows a container inside a container.

    <main class="snap">
      <section class="snap-section"> intro </section>
      <section class="snap-section"> inputs </section>
      <section class="snap-section"> results </section>
    </main>

With facet.js present, a full-viewport .snap auto-upgrades to the
iOS-proven pager (CSS mandatory snap breaks on iOS): the outer never
free-scrolls — a spring (w=11, z=0.9, tuned and final) moves it between
section tops — sections stay one viewport tall and scroll natively
inside themselves, pulling past an edge pages to the neighbour with a
rubber-band hint, a wheel edge-push hops once with a cooldown, and
paging up lands at the previous section's bottom. Gestures starting on
.chart, input or textarea are ignored. The pager dispatches
"facet:section" ({id, index}) on the .snap and exposes
element.facetPager {go, toEl, index, settle}. data-pager="off" opts
out; without JS, plain CSS snapping remains.

### Button

What it is: the action element, in three variants and three sizes, working
identically on <button> and on <a>. What it is for: .btn-primary carries
the screen's one main action in accent-1. .btn-secondary is every other
action, on the accent-2 fill. .btn-ghost is for quiet actions inside busy
areas, invisible until hovered. When to use it: any click that does something. One primary
per screen at most. Navigation dressed as a button is an <a class="btn">.
Put the button's key action name in a data-event attribute as the
analytics hook.

    <button class="btn btn-primary" data-event="calc-run"
            data-tip="Runs the calculation">Calculate</button>

Variants: .btn-primary, .btn-secondary (default for bare .btn), .btn-ghost
Sizes: .btn-small, default, .btn-large
States built in: hover, focus ring, pressed, :disabled

### Icon button

What it is: a square button holding a single icon. What it is for: compact
actions whose meaning the icon plus its tooltip carries — the clear cross
in a number field, a close button, a share arrow. When to use it: when
space is tight and the action is secondary. The icon alone says nothing, so
every icon button carries an aria-label for screen readers and a data-tip
tooltip for everyone else. Combines with any button variant and size.
Icons are inline SVG with stroke="currentColor" so they take the button's
text color in every theme.

    <button class="btn btn-icon" aria-label="Close" data-tip="Close this panel">
      <svg width="20" height="20" viewBox="0 0 20 20" fill="none"
           stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-linecap="round"
           aria-hidden="true"><path d="m5 5 10 10M15 5 5 15"/></svg>
    </button>

### Field + number input

What it is: a labelled field wrapping the calculator-grade number input,
with a clear button inside the field and a words helper under it. What it
is for: money and other large numbers, entered the way the visitor says
them. The input ships prefilled with an example value, clears fully on
the first tap — and never again once the visitor has typed — groups
digits for the locale as they are typed (25,00,000 where lakh and crore
are the norm, 2,500,000 elsewhere), and reads the number back in words
underneath, in the active language: 25 lakh, 2.5 million, 25 लाख. The
locale follows facet.location and the browser, overridable with
data-numbers="indian|western"; the language with data-language. All
behaviours come from facet.js the moment the input carries data-number.
When to use it: every numeric entry in a calculator. Read the plain
number in app code with facet.numberValue(input). For the error state,
put .field-invalid on the field and swap the hint for a .field-error
line.

    <label class="field">
      <span class="field-label">Monthly investment</span>
      <span class="number-input">
        <input type="text" inputmode="numeric" value="25000" data-number
               data-tip="Amount in rupees. First tap clears the example.">
        <button type="button" class="btn btn-ghost btn-icon btn-small number-clear"
                aria-label="Clear" data-tip="Clear this field">&times;</button>
      </span>
      <span class="number-words" aria-live="polite"></span>
      <span class="field-hint">What you invest every month</span>
    </label>

### Slider

What it is: a real range input wearing the theme — hairline track,
accent-3 thumb — paired with a live value readout. What it is for: picking a number
inside a known range where the feel of more-and-less matters more than
exact digits: years, percentages, counts. When to use it: bounded inputs
with sensible ends. When the exact number matters, pair it with or swap it
for the number input. Put an <output> in the field label and facet.js
keeps it showing the live value. Full keyboard support comes free with the
native element.

    <label class="field">
      <span class="field-label">Years <output>10</output></span>
      <input type="range" class="slider" min="1" max="40" value="10"
             data-tip="Drag, or use the arrow keys">
    </label>

### Result block

What it is: a verdict-first result card: one plain-language sentence a
person can repeat at dinner, one big number, then the supporting figures.
What it is for: the centrepiece of a results screen — readable at a
glance, and announced by screen readers on every recalculation when the
block carries aria-live="polite". When to use it: whenever a calculation
produces one headline answer. Leave out the figures list when there is
nothing to support the verdict. For the empty state before any input, show
a muted verdict line alone.

    <section class="result" aria-live="polite">
      <p class="result-verdict">Your money halves in value every 10 years</p>
      <p class="result-number">Rs 46,319</p>
      <dl class="result-figures">
        <div><dt>Total invested</dt><dd>Rs 12,00,000</dd></div>
        <div><dt>Real return</dt><dd>4.1% a year</dd></div>
      </dl>
    </section>

### Description tooltip

What it is: a one-attribute explanation bubble: put data-tip on any element
and the text appears above it on hover and on keyboard focus. Pure CSS, no
JS involved. What it is for: explaining every interactive element in
place — one of the library's core principles. The description doubles as
documentation anyone can read by pointing at the thing. When to use it:
every interactive element ships with one. Add data-tip-below on elements
near the top of the viewport so the bubble drops under them instead of
clipping off the screen.

    <button class="btn" data-tip="Runs the calculation">Calculate</button>

## JavaScript API

facet.js wires itself to data attributes on DOMContentLoaded; pages opt in
by writing HTML, never JS. A tiny public API is exposed as window.facet
for calculator apps:

    facet.set({...})           apply configuration live: theme, mode,
                               density, language, or any --token override
    facet.now()                { date, iso, dateText, timeText, timezone,
                               year } — synchronous, fresh every call
    facet.location             null until resolved, then { city, region,
                               country, countryCode, latitude, longitude,
                               source } from an IP lookup (default
                               ipwho.is; override or disable with
                               data-location-endpoint on the script tag),
                               upgraded to GPS only where permission was
                               already granted. facet.locate() is the
                               promise behind it. Never throws.
    facet.numberValue(input)   the plain number behind a grouped input
    facet.groupNumber(n)       grouped for the active locale
    facet.groupIndian(n)       74250000 -> "7,42,50,000", always Indian
    facet.numberWords(n)       "7.43 crore" / "74.25 million" / "7.43
                               करोड़" per locale and language
    facet.numberSystem()       "indian" or "western", however resolved
    facet.strings              the translation table; extend in place
    facet.install()            show the install prompt when available
    facet.go(url)              navigate with the page transition

## Page transitions

Put data-transition="page" on the html tag (or the facet.js script tag,
or facet.set({transition: "page"})) and every same-origin navigation
between Facet pages transitions: the old page eases up and away, the
new one springs in per the motion personality, and the URL changes
normally. A link with data-no-transition snaps out instead. facet.go(url)
navigates programmatically with the same transition. Built on
cross-document view transitions; browsers without support simply
navigate. data-motion="off" and prefers-reduced-motion make pages snap.

## Offline, install and updates

Create sw.js at your project root with exactly one line:

    importScripts("https://[domain]/lib/facet-sw.js");

(The browser requires the registered file to be same-origin; the line is
a pointer, not a copy — the strategy lives in the library and updates
centrally.) Then opt in on the script tag:

    <script src="https://[domain]/lib/facet.js"
            data-service-worker="/sw.js" defer></script>

Pages and the /lib/ files are then network-first (per the caching law:
every refresh loads the current deploy, the cache answers only offline)
while other static assets serve from cache instantly and revalidate in
the background. Add <link rel="manifest" href="/manifest.json">
using /templates/manifest.json (name, colors matched to your theme's
--background, icons 192 / 512 / 512-maskable), give any button
data-install, and the button appears exactly when installation is
available. Until v1, the library itself is always the latest from the
web; versioned freezes start at /lib/v1/.

## Backgrounds

Whisper-faint page and section backgrounds drawn with CSS gradients
only, colored by the tokens so every theme and mode gets them for
free. One class on a section or body; content sits straight on top.
.bg-grid is the technical drawing (plus marks at grid intersections),
.bg-dots the dot grid, .bg-ruled writing lines, .bg-graph graph paper
with minor and major lines. All fade to nothing under
prefers-contrast: more.

    <section class="bg-grid"> ... </section>

## Themed map

A Google Map wearing the active theme — ground, water, parks and roads
derived from the base family, labels from the text family, boundaries
from the quiet accent, recomputed live when the theme or mode switches.
Bring your own key (the library never ships one): put data-map on a
div, the key in data-map-key (or data-maps-key on the facet.js script
tag), optional data-lat / data-lng / data-zoom. Without a key, or
offline, the div stays a quiet themed placeholder — never a broken
embed. facet.mapStyle() returns the styles array for anything else.
Styles derive from tokens by formula, never hand-tuned per theme, so
every future theme gets its map for free.

    <div data-map data-lat="28.6129" data-lng="77.2295" data-zoom="12"
         data-map-key="YOUR_KEY"></div>

## The app kit (ingested from the shipped inflation app)

Tooltip on touch: the [data-tip] bubble gains a triangle tail, and on
touch devices (where iOS makes :hover stick) a tap peeks the bubble for
five seconds instead — automatic, nothing to wire. Pinch zoom is
blocked at the gesture level.

Choice grid: .choice-grid of .choice-btn (a .choice-sym above a
.choice-cap), five per row; aria-pressed carries the pick and facet.js
keeps it exclusive with a tick of feedback. For any single pick from up
to ~10 symbolic options.

Slider tick scale: wrap a .slider and a .slider-scale in a
.slider-wrap; facet.js builds minor ticks (data-minor, default 10),
sparse labels (data-label, default 40) and a highlighted data-now value
from the slider's own min/max. facet.sliderScale(wrap) rebuilds after a
range change.

Install nudge card (.nudge-scrim > .nudge-card) and the full-screen
instruction overlay (.overlay-guide): the gentle add-to-home-screen ask
and the dark how-to screen, driven by facet.installNudge — addNow()
returns "native" (Android prompt shown) or "guide" (show the overlay).
The overlay's dark palette is deliberately fixed across themes.

Components: .tab-bar (bottom pill of .tab-seg segments + .tab-divider +
trailing .tab-settings gear; a .tab-indicator pill is injected and
glides on per-edge analytic springs), .sheet-scrim + .sheet
(right-edge settings panel on a rigid rubber-band spring; scrim tap and
Esc close), .menu-item/.menu-label/.menu-icon-row/.menu-icon-btn (thin
menu rows and square icon toggles), .scroll-gauge (floating scrollbar
look-alike; .scroll-gauge-page is the draggable whole-page lane), and
.float-btn — a lone floating round trigger pinned bottom-left
(.float-btn-right for bottom-right): give it aria-controls pointing at
a .sheet and it opens it, aria-expanded managed, press feedback
included. Markup contracts: the wall entries on the homepage — the
copy button under each demo gives the exact HTML.

Modules on window.facet: feedback (synthesized UI sounds + haptics, ON
by default; feedback.sound.enabled / feedback.haptic.enabled are the
switches), sheet(el, scrim, {onChange}), scrollGauge(scroller, {...}),
tabIndicator(bar), installNudge(el, {...}), motion (parallax engine:
register/init/setMode/cycle, modes off|cursor|tilt, reduced-motion
off). Auto-wiring: .tab-bar finds the page's pager and its sheet; any
button[aria-controls] on a .sheet opens it. Velvet law: elements with
velvet lift/press transforms must not register with facet.motion.

## Rules for building on Facet

Core principles: readability is the product. One-glance HTML. Not one
extra wrapper. Least code that stays readable. Pure HTML, CSS and JS, no
build step, never minified. One-step theme change. Everything explained in
place. Fully commented. Accessible, SEO-ready and AI-crawlable by default.

Markup rules: semantic tags over div (header, nav, main, section, article,
aside, footer, figure). The wrapper law: an element exists only to hold
content, create layout, or carry behavior; anything else is deleted.
Container inside a container only to enable a real feature: scroll areas,
sticky regions, overflow clipping. Buttons are button, links are a, form
fields have real label elements. One h1 per page; heading levels never
skip.

Adding a new component, the compliance checklist: semantic tokens only.
Works in every theme, light and dark, with zero extra code. All states
covered. Keyboard operable with a visible focus ring. Interactive elements
ship with a description tooltip. 44px touch targets, no hover-only
interactions. Minimal semantic markup. Fully commented in the file. Names
follow the naming rules, nothing minified. data-event analytics hook on
the key action. Wall entry on the library homepage: live demo, variant and
state chips, exact snippet with copy. Docs description — what it is, what
it is for, when to use it — same text as the file comment and this file.
Committed to Git with a clear message.

Analytics: no vendor is baked in. Every key action carries a stable,
documented data-event name — the same hooks agents target. Five lines
wire any tool to all of them at once:

    <script>
      addEventListener("click", (event) => {
        const hook = event.target.closest("[data-event]");
        if (hook) yourAnalytics.track(hook.dataset.event, { page: location.pathname });
      });
    </script>

Naming: tokens by role, not value. One class prefix and pattern per
component (.btn, .btn-primary). JS: one small named function per behavior.

Maintaining: docs are demos — a component is not done until the homepage
shows it live with its code. One component, one commented section in the
CSS. All changes go through Git commits. Growth by extraction: build a new
pattern inside a project first, promote it once it repeats. App logic,
data fetching and state management live in projects, never in the library.
This file is updated whenever a component or rule changes.

Distribution: consume by URL only. Releases are Git tags. When a project
ships, freeze a copy of the current files to /lib/v1/ and leave them
untouched; work continues in /lib/. Old projects point at their frozen
folder forever.

## Platform laws — paid for on iOS

Hard-won facts from shipped app work, mostly against mobile Safari. They
bind everything built with Facet; the library already obeys each one, so
following them costs nothing — the laws are here so nobody relearns them
the expensive way.

Pager law: full-page snap sections that can outgrow the viewport are
never built with CSS scroll-snap — mandatory re-snaps a tall section
back to its top after a flick and wedges the next gesture, proximity
feels like no snap, and a free-scrolling outer with a JS settle-snap is
mushy and fights momentum. The model that works is the pager .snap
ships: the outer never free-scrolls, JS springs its scrollTop between
section tops, each section is 100dvh and scrolls natively inside, and
gestures hand off at section edges with a rubber-band hint.

Gesture law: tame iOS with touch-action — pan-y on scrollers (vertical
scroll only, also kills double-tap zoom and pinch), manipulation on
fixed chrome, none on drag surfaces such as scrubbing charts and the
page gauge. Pair with a gesturestart preventDefault for older iOS pinch
(facet.js does this globally). Never rely on viewport user-scalable=no
or maximum-scale; iOS ignores both.

App shell law: for a standalone PWA, viewport-fit=cover alone is not
enough on iOS — without apple-mobile-web-app-capable and
apple-mobile-web-app-status-bar-style set to black-translucent, the
installed app gets an opaque strip cutting the page at the status bar.
Every app page carries both metas, keeps safe-area env() insets in its
section padding, and the manifest's background_color and theme_color
match the shipped theme.

Caching law: HTML documents and, until versioned freezes exist, the
/lib/ files themselves are never cache-first — every refresh loads from
the network and caches answer only offline. A worker that serves pages
from cache and revalidates behind makes every deploy invisible until a
second refresh; that bug shipped once and is now law. facet-sw.js
implements this: network-first for pages and /lib/, cache-first with
background revalidation for other static assets.

Parallax exclusion: elements carrying their own transform physics —
velvet lift and press, the pager's rubber-band translate — never
register with facetMotion. Two writers on one inline transform collide,
and the loser flickers.

Font-list law: never put inherit inside a font-family list
(ui-rounded, "SF Pro Rounded", inherit is silently invalid and drops
the whole declaration). A stack ends in a generic family, always.

## iOS, honestly: what a website can and cannot do

What a website CAN do on iOS: tint Safari's chrome with theme-color
(facet.js keeps the meta synced to the active theme's background,
live); ship apple-touch-icon and install standalone (manifest + the
apple metas from the app shell law); send web push and set an app
badge — but only after the visitor adds the app to their home screen
(iOS 16.4+), never from a plain Safari tab, and only from a user
gesture. What it CANNOT do: see or join Screen Time, read a visitor's
age, or apply parental controls — OS territory; do not design around
it. Child safety has no web API: the real levers are self-labeling
(adult content declares itself with the RTA rating meta,
<meta name="rating" content="RTA-5042-1996-1400-1577-RTA">, so OS
filters catch it; family content states isFamilyFriendly in JSON-LD)
and restraint — nothing Facet ships autoplays, hooks, or dark-patterns
anyone, children included.

The head pack every Facet app page carries:

    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1, viewport-fit=cover">
    <meta name="theme-color" content="#FFFFFF">  <!-- facet.js keeps this synced -->
    <meta name="apple-mobile-web-app-capable" content="yes">
    <meta name="apple-mobile-web-app-status-bar-style" content="black-translucent">
    <link rel="apple-touch-icon" href="/icons/icon-180.png">
    <link rel="manifest" href="/manifest.json">
